AI.tldr: The paper introduces a hybrid approach to hate speech detection using LLMs and curated vocabularies to identify and explain harmful content in English, French, and Greek.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the lack of transparency and accountability in automated hate speech detection systems, which have traditionally focused more on censorship than explanation.
AI.method: It employs a hybrid system combining LLMs with curated vocabularies. Two pipelines are used: one for identifying problematic terms through the vocabularies, and another for using LLMs to evaluate the context of group-targeted content. Outputs are fused into grounded explanations.
AI.result: The hybrid approach accurately detects hate speech and provides high-quality explanations, surpassing LLM-only systems in human evaluations.
AI.conclusion: By enhancing both detection and explainability, the approach improves transparency, accountability, and the informative nature of hate speech detection systems.

title: Beyond Visual Cues: Semantic-Driven Token Filtering and Expert Routing for Anytime Person ReID
summary: Any-Time Person Re-identification (AT-ReID) necessitates the robust retrieval of target individuals under arbitrary conditions, encompassing both modality shifts (daytime and nighttime) and extensive clothing-change scenarios, ranging from short-term to long-term intervals. However, existing methods are highly relying on pure visual features, which are prone to change due to environmental and time factors, resulting in significantly performance deterioration under scenarios involving illumination caused modality shifts or cloth-change. In this paper, we propose Semantic-driven Token Filtering and Expert Routing (STFER), a novel framework that leverages the ability of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) to generate identity consistency text, which provides identity-discriminative features that are robust to both clothing variations and cross-modality shifts between RGB and IR. Specifically, we employ instructions to guide the LVLM in generating identity-intrinsic semantic text that captures biometric constants for the semantic model driven. The text token is further used for Semantic-driven Visual Token Filtering (SVTF), which enhances informative visual regions and suppresses redundant background noise. Meanwhile, the text token is also used for Semantic-driven Expert Routing (SER), which integrates the semantic text into expert routing, resulting in more robust multi-scenario gating. Extensive experiments on the Any-Time ReID dataset (AT-USTC) demonstrate that our model achieves state-of-the-art results. Moreover, the model trained on AT-USTC was evaluated across 5 widely-used ReID benchmarks demonstrating superior generalization capabilities with highly competitive results. Our code will be available soon.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces STFER, a person re-identification model enhancing identity retrieval across modality shifts and clothing changes using semantic-driven techniques.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the challenge of robust person re-identification in scenarios with modality shifts (e.g., day/night) and changes in clothing, as existing methods relying on visual features experience performance deterioration.
AI.method: The proposed framework, STFER, utilizes large vision-language models (LVLMs) to generate identity-consistent semantic text to improve identity-discriminative feature extraction. Techniques include Semantic-driven Visual Token Filtering (SVTF) for refining visual regions and Semantic-driven Expert Routing (SER) for robust gating across scenarios.
AI.result: Experiments conducted on the AT-USTC dataset highlight the state-of-the-art performance of STFER, with further evaluations showing strong generalization capabilities across five other prominent ReID benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed model demonstrates its robustness across diverse conditions, confirming its potential for improved person re-identification in challenging scenarios involving clothing and modality variations.

title: Assessing the Potential of Masked Autoencoder Foundation Models in Predicting Downhole Metrics from Surface Drilling Data
summary: Oil and gas drilling operations generate extensive time-series data from surface sensors, yet accurate real-time prediction of critical downhole metrics remains challenging due to the scarcity of labelled downhole measurements. This systematic mapping study reviews thirteen papers published between 2015 and 2025 to assess the potential of Masked Autoencoder Foundation Models (MAEFMs) for predicting downhole metrics from surface drilling data. The review identifies eight commonly collected surface metrics and seven target downhole metrics. Current approaches predominantly employ neural network architectures such as artificial neural networks (ANNs) and long short-term memory (LSTM) networks, yet no studies have explored MAEFMs despite their demonstrated effectiveness in time-series modeling. MAEFMs offer distinct advantages through self-supervised pre-training on abundant unlabeled data, enabling multi-task prediction and improved generalization across wells. This research establishes that MAEFMs represent a technically feasible but unexplored opportunity for drilling analytics, recommending future empirical validation of their performance against existing models and exploration of their broader applicability in oil and gas operations.

AI.tldr: This study reviews 13 papers exploring the potential of Masked Autoencoder Foundation Models (MAEFMs) for predicting oil and gas drilling downhole metrics from surface data, identifying it as a promising but unexplored approach.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of accurately predicting critical downhole metrics during oil and gas drilling due to scarce labeled measurements and evaluate MAEFMs as a potential solution.
AI.method: The authors conducted a systematic review of 13 papers (2015-2025), identifying common surface metrics, target downhole metrics, and analyzing current approaches like ANNs and LSTMs for time-series modeling.
AI.result: MAEFMs, known for their strengths in time-series modeling, were found to be unexplored in drilling analytics despite offering advantages like self-supervised learning and improved generalization.
AI.conclusion: MAEFMs are technically feasible for drilling analytics but require future empirical validation and exploration of their broader potential application in oil and gas operations.

title: Predicting Power-System Dynamic Trajectories with Foundation Models
summary: As power systems transition toward renewable-rich and inverter-dominated operations, accurate time-domain dynamic analysis becomes increasingly critical. Such analysis supports key operational tasks, including transient stability assessment, dynamic security analysis, contingency screening, and post-fault trajectory evaluation. In practice, these tasks may operate under several challenges, including unknown and time-varying system parameters, privacy constraints on data sharing, and the need for fast online inference. Existing learning-based approaches are typically trained for individual systems and therefore lack generalization across operating conditions and physical parameters. Hence, this paper proposes LArge Scale Small ODE (LASS)-ODE-Power, a learning framework for general-purpose time-domain prediction. The proposed approach leverages large-scale pretraining on more than 40 GB of DAE or ordinary differential-equation (ODE) trajectories to learn transferable representations. The resulting model supports trajectory prediction from short measurement prefixes across diverse dynamic regimes, including electromechanical and inverter-driven systems. Hence, the model can be directly used without data sharing in a zero-shot setting. In addition, the proposed architecture incorporates parallel and linearized computation to achieve fast inference. Moreover, to enhance task-specific performance in power systems, a specialized fine-tuning strategy is developed based on approximately 1 GB of heterogeneous power-system dynamic data. Extensive experiments over diverse power-system simulation scenarios demonstrate that LASS-ODE-Power consistently outperforms existing learning-based models in trajectory prediction accuracy with efficient inference.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the LASS-ODE-Power framework for time-domain prediction in renewable-dominated power systems, achieving high accuracy and fast inference through large-scale pretraining and fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address challenges in time-domain dynamic analysis for renewable-dominated power systems, where traditional approaches fail to generalize across varying system conditions and parameters.
AI.method: The authors propose a learning framework, LASS-ODE-Power, that uses large-scale pretraining with over 40 GB of data and a specialized fine-tuning strategy with about 1 GB of data. The model incorporates parallel computation and linearization for fast and efficient trajectory prediction.
AI.result: LASS-ODE-Power outperforms existing models in trajectory prediction across varied power-system scenarios, achieving superior accuracy and efficient online inference.
AI.conclusion: This framework provides a generalized and efficient solution for dynamic analysis in renewable-rich power systems, overcoming existing limitations like lack of generalization and need for data sharing.

title: IUQ: Interrogative Uncertainty Quantification for Long-Form Large Language Model Generation
summary: Despite the rapid advancement of Large Language Models (LLMs), uncertainty quantification in LLM generation is a persistent challenge. Although recent approaches have achieved strong performance by restricting LLMs to produce short or constrained answer sets, many real-world applications require long-form and free-form text generation. A key difficulty in this setting is that LLMs often produce responses that are semantically coherent yet factually inaccurate, while the underlying semantics are multifaceted and the linguistic structure is complex. To tackle this challenge, this paper introduces Interrogative Uncertainty Quantification (IUQ), a novel framework that leverages inter-sample consistency and intra-sample faithfulness to quantify the uncertainty in long-form LLM outputs. By utilizing an interrogate-then-respond paradigm, our method provides reliable measures of claim-level uncertainty and the model's faithfulness. Experimental results across diverse model families and model sizes demonstrate the superior performance of IUQ over two widely used long-form generation datasets. The code is available at https://github.com/louisfanhz/IUQ.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Interrogative Uncertainty Quantification (IUQ), a method to assess uncertainty in long-form text generation by LLMs, addressing challenges of coherence and factual accuracy.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of uncertainty quantification in LLM-generated long-form, free-form text, where outputs can be semantically coherent but factually inaccurate.
AI.method: The paper proposes IUQ, a framework using an interrogate-then-respond paradigm to assess uncertainty based on inter-sample consistency and intra-sample faithfulness.
AI.result: Experimental results show IUQ's superior uncertainty quantification performance across various LLM families and sizes on benchmark datasets.
AI.conclusion: IUQ is a reliable framework for quantifying uncertainty in LLM long-form outputs, offering improved claim-level reliability and faithfulness assessment.

title: Beyond Independent Frames: Latent Attention Masked Autoencoders for Multi-View Echocardiography
comment: Accepted as a workshop paper at the ICLR 2026 Workshop on Foundation Models for Science
summary: Echocardiography is a widely used modality for cardiac assessment due to its non-invasive and cost-effective nature, but the sparse and heterogeneous spatiotemporal views of the heart pose distinct challenges. Existing masked autoencoder (MAE) approaches typically process images or short clips independently, failing to capture the inherent multi-view structure required for coherent cardiac representation. We introduce Latent Attention Masked Autoencoder (LAMAE), a foundation model architecture tailored to the multi-view nature of medical imaging. LAMAE augments the standard MAE with a latent attention module that enables information exchange across frames and views directly in latent space. This allows the model to aggregate variable-length sequences and distinct views, reconstructing a holistic representation of cardiac function from partial observations. We pretrain LAMAE on MIMIC-IV-ECHO, a large-scale, uncurated dataset reflecting real-world clinical variability. To the best of our knowledge, we present the first results for predicting ICD-10 codes from MIMIC-IV-ECHO videos. Furthermore, we empirically demonstrate that representations learned from adult data transfer effectively to pediatric cohorts despite substantial anatomical differences. These results provide evidence that incorporating structural priors, such as multi-view attention, yields significantly more robust and transferable representations.

AI.tldr: LAMAE model enhances cardiac imaging by applying latent attention in autoencoders for improved recognition and transferability.
AI.motivation: To overcome challenges in cardiac imaging due to sparse and heterogeneous views in echocardiography.
AI.method: Developed LAMAE, a model that integrates latent attention to process multi-view medical imaging data effectively.
AI.result: LAMAE improves cardiac representation using echocardiographic data and successfully predicts ICD-10 codes.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating multi-view attention enhances robustness and transferability of learned representations across varying patient datasets.

title: MambaSL: Exploring Single-Layer Mamba for Time Series Classification
summary: Despite recent advances in state space models (SSMs) such as Mamba across various sequence domains, research on their standalone capacity for time series classification (TSC) has remained limited. We propose MambaSL, a framework that minimally redesigns the selective SSM and projection layers of a single-layer Mamba, guided by four TSC-specific hypotheses. To address benchmarking limitations -- restricted configurations, partial University of East Anglia (UEA) dataset coverage, and insufficiently reproducible setups -- we re-evaluate 20 strong baselines across all 30 UEA datasets under a unified protocol. As a result, MambaSL achieves state-of-the-art performance with statistically significant average improvements, while ensuring reproducibility via public checkpoints for all evaluated models. Together with visualizations, these results demonstrate the potential of Mamba-based architectures as a TSC backbone.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MambaSL, an improved framework based on Mamba, achieving state-of-the-art performance in time series classification (TSC) with better benchmarking and reproducibility.
AI.motivation: To address the limited research on state space models (SSMs) for standalone time series classification and to set a standardized benchmarking protocol for evaluating such models.
AI.method: MambaSL minimally redesigns Mamba's selective SSM and projection layers, tests hypotheses specific to TSC, and re-evaluates baselines across all 30 UEA datasets under a unified protocol.
AI.result: MambaSL achieves state-of-the-art performance with statistically significant improvements and ensures reproducibility of results with public checkpoints.
AI.conclusion: Mamba-based architectures are highly effective backbones for TSC, providing robust performance and setting new standards in benchmarking and reproducibility.

title: COEVO: Co-Evolutionary Framework for Joint Functional Correctness and PPA Optimization in LLM-Based RTL Generation
summary: LLM-based RTL code generation methods increasingly target both functional correctness and PPA quality, yet existing approaches universally decouple the two objectives, optimizing PPA only after correctness is fully achieved. Whether through sequential multi-agent pipelines, evolutionary search with binary correctness gates, or hierarchical reward dependencies, partially correct but architecturally promising candidates are systematically discarded. Moreover, existing methods reduce the multi-objective PPA space to a single scalar fitness, obscuring the trade-offs among area, delay, and power. To address these limitations, we propose COEVO, a co-evolutionary framework that unifies correctness and PPA optimization within a single evolutionary loop. COEVO formulates correctness as a continuous co-optimization dimension alongside area, delay, and power, enabled by an enhanced testbench that provides fine-grained scoring and detailed diagnostic feedback. An adaptive correctness gate with annealing allows PPA-promising but partially correct candidates to guide the search toward jointly optimal solutions. To preserve the full PPA trade-off structure, COEVO employs four-dimensional Pareto-based non-dominated sorting with configurable intra-level sorting, replacing scalar fitness without manual weight tuning. Evaluated on VerilogEval 2.0 and RTLLM 2.0, COEVO achieves 97.5\% and 94.5\% Pass@1 with GPT-5.4-mini, surpassing all agentic baselines across four LLM backbones, while attaining the best PPA on 43 out of 49 synthesizable RTLLM designs.

AI.tldr: COEVO integrates correctness and PPA optimization in RTL code generation within a unified evolutionary framework, achieving superior functional and PPA outcomes.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for RTL code generation treat correctness and PPA optimization separately, often discarding promising candidates that are only partially correct, and reducing multi-objective optimization to a single scalar fitness.
AI.method: COEVO unifies correctness and PPA optimization by employing co-evolution with fine-grained scoring, an adaptive correctness gate with annealing, and Pareto-based non-dominated sorting to preserve trade-offs across area, delay, and power.
AI.result: When tested on VerilogEval 2.0 and RTLLM 2.0 with multiple LLM backbones, COEVO achieved Pass@1 scores of 97.5% and 94.5% while delivering industry-leading PPA performance on 43 out of 49 designs.
AI.conclusion: COEVO successfully integrates correctness and PPA optimization, outperforming established baselines in both functional correctness and PPA metrics, making it a robust framework for automated RTL code generation.

title: Blinded Multi-Rater Comparative Evaluation of a Large Language Model and Clinician-Authored Responses in CGM-Informed Diabetes Counseling
summary: Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) is central to diabetes care, but explaining CGM patterns clearly and empathetically remains time-intensive. Evidence for retrieval-grounded large language model (LLM) systems in CGM-informed counseling remains limited. To evaluate whether a retrieval-grounded LLM-based conversational agent (CA) could support patient understanding of CGM data and preparation for routine diabetes consultations. We developed a retrieval-grounded LLM-based CA for CGM interpretation and diabetes counseling support. The system generated plain-language responses while avoiding individualized therapeutic advice. Twelve CGM-informed cases were constructed from publicly available datasets. Between Oct 2025 and Feb 2026, 6 senior UK diabetes clinicians each reviewed 2 assigned cases and answered 24 questions. In a blinded multi-rater evaluation, each CA-generated and clinician-authored response was independently rated by 3 clinicians on 6 quality dimensions. Safety flags and perceived source labels were also recorded. Primary analyses used linear mixed-effects models. A total of 288 unique responses (144 CA and 144 clinician) generated 864 ratings. The CA received higher quality scores than clinician responses (mean 4.37 vs 3.58), with an estimated mean difference of 0.782 points (95% CI 0.692-0.872; P<.001). The largest differences were for empathy (1.062, 95% CI 0.948-1.177) and actionability (0.992, 95% CI 0.877-1.106). Safety flag distributions were similar, with major concerns rare in both groups (3/432, 0.7% each). Retrieval-grounded LLM systems may have value as adjunct tools for CGM review, patient education, and preconsultation preparation. However, these findings do not support autonomous therapeutic decision-making or unsupervised real-world use.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the use of a retrieval-grounded large language model (LLM) conversational agent (CA) for supporting diabetes care by evaluating its ability to interpret continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) data and assist in patient education.
AI.motivation: The study aims to address challenges in communicating CGM patterns empathetically and effectively, which are essential but time-intensive tasks in diabetes care. Given the growing interest in using LLM systems for healthcare applications, this research seeks to explore their potential benefit in diabetes counseling.
AI.method: A retrieval-grounded LLM-based conversational agent was developed for interpreting CGM data and generating plain-language responses without providing individualized therapeutic advice. The model's outputs were compared to clinician-authored responses, based on quality ratings across various dimensions by senior diabetes clinicians.
AI.result: Clinician-author responses received lower quality scores compared to CA-generated responses across multiple dimensions, notably empathy and actionability. Safety concerns were found to be rare and comparable between the two groups, with both achieving minimal safety flags.
AI.conclusion: Retrieval-grounded LLM-based systems show promise as tools for CGM data review, patient education, and preconsultation preparation. However, they are not recommended for autonomous therapeutic decision-making or unsupervised implementation.

title: How to Correctly Make Mistakes: A Framework for Constructing and Benchmarking Mistake Aware Egocentric Procedural Videos
summary: Reliable procedural monitoring in video requires exposure to naturally occurring human errors and the recoveries that follow. In egocentric recordings, mistakes are often partially occluded by hands and revealed through subtle object state changes, while existing procedural datasets provide limited and inconsistent mistake and correction traces. We present PIE-V (Psychologically Inspired Error injection for Videos), a framework for constructing and benchmarking mistake-aware egocentric procedural videos by augmenting clean keystep procedures with controlled, human-plausible deviations. PIE-V combines a psychology-informed error planner conditioned on procedure phase and semantic step load, a correction planner that models recovery behavior, an LLM writer that performs cascade-consistent rewrites, and an LLM judge that validates procedural coherence and repairs failures. For video segment edits, PIE-V synthesizes replacement clips with text-guided video generation and stitches them into the episode to preserve visual plausibility. Applied to 17 tasks and 50 Ego-Exo4D scenarios, PIE-V injects 102 mistakes and generates 27 recovery corrections. For benchmarking, we introduce a unified taxonomy and a human rubric with nine metrics that cover step-level and procedure-level quality, including plausibility, procedure logic with annotator confidence, state change coherence, and grounding between text and video. Using this protocol, we audit several existing resources and compare PIE-V against a freeform LLM generation baseline under the same criteria. Together, the framework and rubric support post-completion verification for egocentric procedural mistake detection and correction.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PIE-V, a framework for generating and analyzing mistake-aware egocentric procedural videos, combining psychology-guided error modeling, LLM-based validation, and text-guided video edits.
AI.motivation: Human errors and corrections in egocentric procedural videos are poorly represented in current datasets, limiting reliability in procedural monitoring applications.
AI.method: PIE-V uses psychology-based error planning, LLMs for procedural validation, and video synthesis techniques to integrate controlled errors and corrections into egocentric task sequences.
AI.result: PIE-V generated 102 unique mistakes and 27 recovery corrections applied to 17 tasks across 50 scenarios, and benchmarked using a taxonomy and human rubric with nine evaluation metrics.
AI.conclusion: PIE-V enhances procedural monitoring by simulating realistic errors and corrections, addressing gaps in egocentric datasets, while its benchmarking rubric supports assessment of mistake-aware procedural systems.

title: AdaSplash-2: Faster Differentiable Sparse Attention
summary: Sparse attention has been proposed as a way to alleviate the quadratic cost of transformers, a central bottleneck in long-context training. A promising line of work is $α$-entmax attention, a differentiable sparse alternative to softmax that enables input-dependent sparsity yet has lagged behind softmax due to the computational overhead necessary to compute the normalizer $τ$. In this paper, we introduce AdaSplash-2, which addresses this limitation through a novel histogram-based initialization that reduces the number of iterations needed to compute $τ$ to typically 1--2. The key idea is to compute a coarse histogram of attention scores on the fly and store it in on-chip SRAM, yielding a more accurate initialization that enables fast forward and backward computation. Combined with a sparsity-aware GPU implementation that skips zero blocks with low overhead, AdaSplash-2 matches or improves per-step training time relative to FlashAttention-2 when block sparsity is moderate-to-high (e.g., $>$60\%), which often occurs at long-context lengths. On downstream tasks, models trained with our efficient $α$-entmax attention match softmax baselines at short-context lengths and achieve substantial gains in long-context settings.

AI.tldr: AdaSplash-2 improves sparse attention computational efficiency in long-context transformers via a histogram-based approach and GPU optimizations, enabling better performance and faster training.
AI.motivation: To address the quadratic cost and computational inefficiencies of transformers for long-context sequences using sparse attention methods.
AI.method: Introduced AdaSplash-2, which employs a histogram-based initialization to compute the normalizer τ in fewer iterations. It also utilizes a sparsity-aware GPU implementation for computational efficiency.
AI.result: AdaSplash-2 achieves faster training times relative to FlashAttention-2 at high sparsity levels (> 60%) and improves downstream task performance, particularly for long-context training.
AI.conclusion: AdaSplash-2 provides an efficient solution to sparse attention computation, with significant performance gains in long-context model training scenarios.

title: Towards Faster Language Model Inference Using Mixture-of-Experts Flow Matching
summary: Flow matching retains the generation quality of diffusion models while enabling substantially faster inference, making it a compelling paradigm for generative modeling. However, when applied to language modeling, it exhibits fundamental limitations in representing complex latent distributions with irregular geometries, such as anisotropy and multimodality. To address these challenges, we propose a mixture-of-experts flow matching (MoE-FM) framework, which captures complex global transport geometries in latent space by decomposing them into locally specialized vector fields. Building on MoE-FM, we develop a non-autoregressive (NAR) language modeling approach, named YAN, instantiated with both Transformer and Mamba architectures. Across multiple downstream tasks, YAN achieves generation quality on par with both autoregressive (AR) and diffusion-based NAR language models, while requiring as few as three sampling steps. This yields a $40\times$ speedup over AR baselines and up to a $10^3\times$ speedup over diffusion language models, demonstrating substantial efficiency advantages for language modeling.

AI.tldr: Flow matching is adapted for faster language generation but has limitations in handling complex latent spaces. The proposed mixture-of-experts flow matching (MoE-FM) introduces locally specialized vector fields to overcome these limitations. The resulting YAN framework achieves high-quality language generation with significantly faster sampling.
AI.motivation: Current flow-matching models provide fast inference but struggle with representing complex latent distributions in language modeling. This limitation necessitates innovative approaches to maintain generation quality while improving efficiency.
AI.method: The authors propose MoE-FM, which breaks down latent space complexities into locally specialized vector fields, and implement this in the YAN framework using Transformer and Mamba architectures for efficient, high-quality generation in a non-autoregressive manner.
AI.result: YAN matches the quality of autoregressive and diffusion-based models on multiple tasks and achieves a $40\times$ speedup over autoregressive baselines and up to a $10^3\times$ speedup over diffusion models in inference.
AI.conclusion: MoE-FM and the YAN framework successfully address efficiency and quality challenges in language modeling, offering a scalable alternative to traditional methods while maintaining competitive performance.

title: DiscoTrace: Representing and Comparing Answering Strategies of Humans and LLMs in Information-Seeking Question Answering
summary: We introduce DiscoTrace, a method to identify the rhetorical strategies that answerers use when responding to information-seeking questions. DiscoTrace represents answers as a sequence of question-related discourse acts paired with interpretations of the original question, annotated on top of rhetorical structure theory parses. Applying DiscoTrace to answers from nine different human communities reveals that communities have diverse preferences for answer construction. In contrast, LLMs do not exhibit rhetorical diversity in their answers, even when prompted to mimic specific human community answering guidelines. LLMs also systematically opt for breadth, addressing interpretations of questions that human answerers choose not to address. Our findings can guide the development of pragmatic LLM answerers that consider a range of strategies informed by context in QA.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DiscoTrace, a method to analyze rhetorical strategies in answers to information-seeking questions and finds differences in human versus LLM answering styles.
AI.motivation: To understand how human communities and large language models (LLMs) differ in constructing answers to information-seeking questions, and to address limitations in LLM-generated answers.
AI.method: Develop DiscoTrace, a framework for annotating answers based on rhetorical strategies and interpretations of questions using rhetorical structure theory parses, and apply it to human and LLM-generated answers.
AI.result: Human communities exhibit diverse answer styles, while LLMs lack rhetorical diversity and tend toward generalization, addressing interpretations of questions that humans avoid.
AI.conclusion: DiscoTrace demonstrates that pragmatic, context-informed strategies are essential for developing LLMs capable of mimicking diverse human answering styles.

title: KVNN: Learnable Multi-Kernel Volterra Neural Networks
summary: Higher-order learning is fundamentally rooted in exploiting compositional features. It clearly hinges on enriching the representation by more elaborate interactions of the data which, in turn, tends to increase the model complexity of conventional large-scale deep learning models. In this paper, a kernelized Volterra Neural Network (kVNN) is proposed. The key to the achieved efficiency lies in using a learnable multi-kernel representation, where different interaction orders are modeled by distinct polynomial-kernel components with compact, learnable centers, yielding an order-adaptive parameterization. Features are learned by the composition of layers, each of which consists of parallel branches of different polynomial orders, enabling kVNN filters to directly replace standard convolutional kernels within existing architectures. The theoretical results are substantiated by experiments on two representative tasks: video action recognition and image denoising. The results demonstrate favorable performance-efficiency trade-offs: kVNN consistently yields reduced model (parameters) and computational (GFLOPs) complexity with competitive and often improved performance. These results are maintained even when trained from scratch without large-scale pretraining. In summary, we substantiate that structured kernelized higher-order layers offer a practical path to balancing expressivity and computational cost in modern deep networks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a kernelized Volterra Neural Network (kVNN) to achieve efficiency and balance between expressivity and computational cost in deep learning models, demonstrating reduced complexity and improved performance in tasks like video action recognition and image denoising.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to balance expressivity and computational cost in modern deep learning models, addressing the challenge of increasing complexity due to higher-order compositional features in large-scale neural networks.
AI.method: The proposed method introduces kVNN, which employs a learnable multi-kernel representation modeled by distinct polynomial kernels with adaptive parameterization. Features are learned through layer composition with parallel branches of different polynomial orders.
AI.result: Experiments on video action recognition and image denoising validate that kVNN achieves reduced model complexity (parameters and GFLOPs) while maintaining competitive or improved performance, even without large-scale pretraining.
AI.conclusion: The structured kernelized higher-order layers in kVNN offer a practical path for balancing expressivity and computational efficiency, presenting favorable trade-offs in modern deep learning tasks.

title: One-shot learning for the complex dynamical behaviors of weakly nonlinear forced oscillators
summary: Extrapolative prediction of complex nonlinear dynamics remains a central challenge in engineering. This study proposes a one-shot learning method to identify global frequency-response curves from a single excitation time history by learning governing equations. We introduce MEv-SINDy (Multi-frequency Evolutionary Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamics) to infer the governing equations of non-autonomous and multi-frequency systems. The methodology leverages the Generalized Harmonic Balance (GHB) method to decompose complex forced responses into a set of slow-varying evolution equations. We validated the capabilities of MEv-SINDy on two critical Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS). These applications include a nonlinear beam resonator and a MEMS micromirror. Our results show that the model trained on a single point accurately predicts softening/hardening effects and jump phenomena across a wide range of excitation levels. This approach significantly reduces the data acquisition burden for the characterization and design of nonlinear microsystems.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces MEv-SINDy, a one-shot approach to predict nonlinear system dynamics from minimal data, validated on MEMS devices.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of predicting complex nonlinear system behaviors with minimal data acquisition.
AI.method: Introduced MEv-SINDy method, which combines sparse identification of nonlinear dynamics and the Generalized Harmonic Balance method to infer governing equations.
AI.result: Validated on two MEMS devices, showing accurate prediction of nonlinear behaviors like softening, hardening, and jumps from minimal data.
AI.conclusion: MEv-SINDy effectively reduces the data burden for nonlinear system analysis, aiding microsystem design and characterization.

title: Autogenesis: A Self-Evolving Agent Protocol
summary: Recent advances in LLM based agent systems have shown promise in tackling complex, long horizon tasks. However, existing agent protocols (e.g., A2A and MCP) under specify cross entity lifecycle and context management, version tracking, and evolution safe update interfaces, which encourages monolithic compositions and brittle glue code. We introduce \textbf{\textsc{Autogenesis Protocol (AGP)}}, a self evolution protocol that decouples what evolves from how evolution occurs. Its Resource Substrate Protocol Layer (RSPL) models prompts, agents, tools, environments, and memory as protocol registered resources\footnote{Unless otherwise specified, resources refer to instances of the five RSPL entity types: \emph{prompt}, \emph{agent}, \emph{tool}, \emph{environment}, \emph{memory} with agent \emph{outputs}.} with explicit state, lifecycle, and versioned interfaces. Its Self Evolution Protocol Layer (SEPL) specifies a closed loop operator interface for proposing, assessing, and committing improvements with auditable lineage and rollback. Building on \textbf{\textsc{AGP}}, we present \textbf{\textsc{Autogenesis System (AGS)}}, a self-evolving multi-agent system that dynamically instantiates, retrieves, and refines protocol-registered resources during execution. We evaluate \textbf{\textsc{AGS}} on multiple challenging benchmarks that require long horizon planning and tool use across heterogeneous resources. The results demonstrate consistent improvements over strong baselines, supporting the effectiveness of agent resource management and closed loop self evolution.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the Autogenesis Protocol (AGP), allowing better self-evolving agent systems, and demonstrates improvements over existing protocols in benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of existing agent protocols (like A2A and MCP), which under specify key aspects like cross-entity lifecycle management, version tracking, and safe evolution interfaces, resulting in less flexible and robust systems.
AI.method: The paper proposes the AGP, consisting of a Resource Substrate Protocol Layer (RSPL) for modeling resources with explicit state and versioned interfaces, and a Self Evolution Protocol Layer (SEPL) for a closed-loop self-improvement mechanism. It also introduces AGS, a multi-agent system built on AGP for dynamic resource management during execution.
AI.result: AGS is evaluated on difficult benchmarks requiring long-term planning and multi-resource use, showing consistent improvements over strong baseline models.
AI.conclusion: The proposed AGP and AGS enable enhanced self-evolution and resource management in multi-agent systems, addressing significant limitations in previous protocols and demonstrating practical effectiveness.

title: QuantCode-Bench: A Benchmark for Evaluating the Ability of Large Language Models to Generate Executable Algorithmic Trading Strategies
summary: Large language models have demonstrated strong performance on general-purpose programming tasks, yet their ability to generate executable algorithmic trading strategies remains underexplored. Unlike standard code benchmarks, trading-strategy generation requires simultaneous mastery of domain-specific financial logic, knowledge of a specialized API, and the ability to produce code that is not only syntactically correct but also leads to actual trades on historical data. In this work, we present QuantCode-Bench, a benchmark for the systematic evaluation of modern LLMs in generating strategies for the Backtrader framework from textual descriptions in English. The benchmark contains 400 tasks of varying difficulty collected from Reddit, TradingView, StackExchange, GitHub, and synthetic sources. Evaluation is conducted through a multi-stage pipeline that checks syntactic correctness, successful backtest execution, the presence of trades, and semantic alignment with the task description using an LLM judge. We compare state-of-the-art models in two settings: single-turn, where the strategy must be generated correctly on the first attempt, and agentic multi-turn, where the model receives iterative feedback and may repair its errors. We analyze the failure modes across different stages of the pipeline and show that the main limitations of current models are not related to syntax, but rather to the correct operationalization of trading logic, proper API usage, and adherence to task semantics. These findings suggest that trading strategy generation constitutes a distinct class of domain-specific code generation tasks in which success requires not only technical correctness, but also alignment between natural-language descriptions, financial logic, and the observable behavior of the strategy on data.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces QuantCode-Bench, a benchmark to evaluate large language models (LLMs) in generating algorithmic trading strategies for the Backtrader framework from textual descriptions, highlighting challenges in task semantics and domain-specific logic handling.
AI.motivation: To explore and systematically evaluate the ability of LLMs to generate executable algorithmic trading strategies, a domain that requires a blend of syntactic correctness, domain-specific financial knowledge, and alignment with task descriptions.
AI.method: The researchers curated a benchmark dataset of 400 tasks from various sources and evaluated LLMs through a multi-stage pipeline for syntactic accuracy, backtest execution, trade presence, and semantic alignment. They tested models in single-turn and multi-turn settings, identifying key failure modes.
AI.result: Current LLMs face challenges related to executing domain-specific financial logic, proper API handling, and task semantics, with difficulties going beyond syntactic correctness.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights the need for advanced techniques to align LLM-generated code with domain-specific requirements and demonstrates that trading strategy generation is a unique subset of code generation tasks requiring specific skill sets.

title: Class Unlearning via Depth-Aware Removal of Forget-Specific Directions
comment: Accepted to the CVPR 2026 Workshop on Machine Unlearning for Vision (MUV)
summary: Machine unlearning aims to remove targeted knowledge from a trained model without the cost of retraining from scratch. In class unlearning, however, reducing accuracy on forget classes does not necessarily imply true forgetting: forgotten information can remain encoded in internal representations, and apparent forgetting may arise from classifier-head suppression rather than representational removal. We show that existing class-unlearning methods often exhibit weak or negative selectivity, preserve forget-class structure in deep representations, or rely heavily on final-layer bias shifts. We then introduce DAMP (Depth-Aware Modulation by Projection), a one-shot, closed-form weight-surgery method that removes forget-specific directions from a pretrained network without gradient-based optimization. At each stage, DAMP computes class prototypes in the input space of the next learnable operator, extracts forget directions as residuals relative to retain-class prototypes, and applies a projection-based update to reduce downstream sensitivity to those directions. To preserve utility, DAMP uses a parameter-free depth-aware scaling rule derived from probe separability, applying smaller edits in early layers and larger edits in deeper layers. The method naturally extends to multi-class forgetting through low-rank subspace removal. Across MNIST, CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Tiny ImageNet, and across convolutional and transformer architectures, DAMP more closely resembles the retraining gold standard than some of the prior methods, improving selective forgetting while better preserving retain-class performance and reducing residual forget-class structure in deep layers.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the problem of machine unlearning, particularly in class unlearning, and introduces a novel method called DAMP for effective and selective forgetting, outperforming prior methods.
AI.motivation: To develop a method for class unlearning that ensures true forgetting of targeted information while preserving the utility of the remaining model, overcoming limitations in existing methods.
AI.method: The paper introduces DAMP, a one-shot weight-surgery method that eliminates forget-specific directions across all layers of a network. It calculates class prototypes, uses projection-based updates to remove residuals related to forget-classes, and employs a depth-aware scaling mechanism to ensure utility is preserved.
AI.result: The DAMP method significantly improves selective forgetting and better retains performance for preserved classes, when tested across various datasets and architectures, outperforming prior unlearning techniques.
AI.conclusion: DAMP effectively achieves class unlearning by using a principled, closed-form approach that balances forgetting and utility retention, offering a solid alternative to retraining and other existing unlearning methods.

title: RL-STPA: Adapting System-Theoretic Hazard Analysis for Safety-Critical Reinforcement Learning
summary: As reinforcement learning (RL) deployments expand into safety-critical domains, existing evaluation methods fail to systematically identify hazards arising from the black-box nature of neural network enabled policies and distributional shift between training and deployment. This paper introduces Reinforcement Learning System-Theoretic Process Analysis (RL-STPA), a framework that adapts conventional STPA's systematic hazard analysis to address RL's unique challenges through three key contributions: hierarchical subtask decomposition using both temporal phase analysis and domain expertise to capture emergent behaviors, coverage-guided perturbation testing that explores the sensitivity of state-action spaces, and iterative checkpoints that feed identified hazards back into training through reward shaping and curriculum design. We demonstrate RL-STPA in the safety-critical test case of autonomous drone navigation and landing, revealing potential loss scenarios that can be missed by standard RL evaluations. The proposed framework provides practitioners with a toolkit for systematic hazard analysis, quantitative metrics for safety coverage assessment, and actionable guidelines for establishing operational safety bounds. While RL-STPA cannot provide formal guarantees for arbitrary neural policies, it offers a practical methodology for systematically evaluating and improving RL safety and robustness in safety-critical applications where exhaustive verification methods remain intractable.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RL-STPA, a practical methodology to systematically identify and mitigate hazards in reinforcement learning systems for safety-critical applications.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluation methods for reinforcement learning fail to address hazards due to black-box nature of policies and distributional shifts in safety-critical domains.
AI.method: The authors develop RL-STPA with hierarchical subtask decomposition, coverage-guided perturbation testing, and iterative checkpoints for refining RL training.
AI.result: They successfully apply RL-STPA to autonomous drone navigation and landing, identifying potential hazards overlooked by standard evaluations.
AI.conclusion: RL-STPA helps assess RL safety through systematic hazard analysis and offers practitioners tools to enhance robustness, despite not providing formal guarantees for neural policies.

title: From Reactive to Proactive: Assessing the Proactivity of Voice Agents via ProVoice-Bench
summary: Recent advancements in LLM agents are gradually shifting from reactive, text-based paradigms toward proactive, multimodal interaction. However, existing benchmarks primarily focus on reactive responses, overlooking the complexities of proactive intervention and monitoring. To bridge this gap, we introduce ProVoice-Bench, the first evaluation framework specifically designed for proactive voice agents, featuring four novel tasks. By leveraging a multi-stage data synthesis pipeline, we curate 1,182 high-quality samples for rigorous testing. Our evaluation of state-of-the-art Multimodal LLMs reveals a significant performance gap, particularly regarding over-triggering and reasoning capabilities. These findings highlight the limitations of current models and offer a roadmap for developing more natural, context-aware proactive agents.

AI.tldr: ProVoice-Bench is presented as a novel evaluation framework for proactive voice agents, aiming to address shortcomings in existing benchmarks and enhance multimodal interaction capabilities.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks focus on reactive agents and fail to evaluate the complexities of proactive intervention and monitoring, which are vital for natural interaction.
AI.method: A multi-stage data synthesis pipeline is employed to generate 1,182 high-quality samples across four newly proposed tasks for testing and evaluating state-of-the-art Multimodal LLMs.
AI.result: Evaluation results reveal substantial challenges, notably over-triggering and limited reasoning capabilities in current multimodal LLM agents.
AI.conclusion: ProVoice-Bench exposes gaps in current models and proposes a pathway to create more context-aware, proactive voice agents for advanced multimodal interactions.

title: Compressing Sequences in the Latent Embedding Space: $K$-Token Merging for Large Language Models
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) incur significant computational and memory costs when processing long prompts, as full self-attention scales quadratically with input length. Token compression aims to address this challenge by reducing the number of tokens representing inputs. However, existing prompt-compression approaches primarily operate in token space and overlook inefficiencies in the latent embedding space. In this paper, we propose K-Token Merging, a latent-space compression framework that merges each contiguous block of K token embeddings into a single embedding via a lightweight encoder. The compressed sequence is processed by a LoRA-adapted LLM, while generation remains in the original vocabulary. Experiments on structural reasoning (Textualized Tree), sentiment classification (Amazon Reviews), and code editing (CommitPackFT) show that K-Token Merging lies on the Pareto frontier of performance vs. compression, achieving up to 75% input length reduction with minimal performance degradation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces K-Token Merging, a latent-space compression method for large language models to reduce input length while maintaining performance, addressing inefficiencies in prompt processing.
AI.motivation: To address the computational and memory inefficiencies of large language models (LLMs) when handling long prompts due to the quadratic scaling of self-attention, particularly focusing on inefficiencies in the latent embedding space.
AI.method: The authors propose K-Token Merging, which compresses input by merging contiguous blocks of K token embeddings into a single embedding using a lightweight encoder. Compressed sequences are processed by a LoRA-adapted LLM while generation uses the original vocabulary.
AI.result: Experiments on three tasks—structural reasoning, sentiment classification, and code editing—demonstrate up to 75% input length reduction with minimal performance degradation, placing the method on the Pareto frontier of performance versus compression.
AI.conclusion: K-Token Merging offers an efficient latent-space compression method that reduces computational costs of LLMs for long prompts, achieving significant input length reduction without sacrificing much performance.

title: OmniLight: One Model to Rule All Lighting Conditions
comment: CVPRW 2026; NTIRE 2026 Image Shadow Removal & Ambient Lighting Normalization Challenges (1st Perceptual Rank for White Lighting, 2nd Fidelity Rank & 4th Perceptual Rank for Color Lighting)
summary: Adverse lighting conditions, such as cast shadows and irregular illumination, pose significant challenges to computer vision systems by degrading visibility and color fidelity. Consequently, effective shadow removal and ALN are critical for restoring underlying image content, improving perceptual quality, and facilitating robust performance in downstream tasks. However, while achieving state-of-the-art results on specific benchmarks is a primary goal in image restoration challenges, real-world applications often demand robust models capable of handling diverse domains. To address this, we present a comprehensive study on lighting-related image restoration by exploring two contrasting strategies. We leverage a robust framework for ALN, DINOLight, as a specialized baseline to exploit the characteristics of each individual dataset, and extend it to OmniLight, a generalized alternative incorporating our proposed Wavelet Domain Mixture-of-Experts (WD-MoE) that is trained across all provided datasets. Through a comparative analysis of these two methods, we discuss the impact of data distribution on the performance of specialized and unified architectures in lighting-related image restoration. Notably, both approaches secured top-tier rankings across all three lighting-related tracks in the NTIRE 2026 Challenge, demonstrating their outstanding perceptual quality and generalization capabilities. Our codes are available at https://github.com/OBAKSA/Lighting-Restoration.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the challenges of lighting-related image restoration. It compares two strategies: a specialized model, DINOLight, and a generalized model, OmniLight, with a novel WD-MoE method. Both models achieved notable results in the NTIRE 2026 Challenge.
AI.motivation: The motivation behind this study is to overcome the challenges posed by adverse lighting conditions, such as shadows and irregular illumination, which impair the performance of computer vision systems. The authors aim to enhance image restoration methods for better real-world applications.
AI.method: The paper utilizes two approaches: DINOLight, a specialized framework tailored to individual datasets, and OmniLight, a generalized model incorporating a Wavelet Domain Mixture-of-Experts (WD-MoE) module trained on multiple datasets. It also provides comparative analysis across these methods.
AI.result: Both DINOLight and OmniLight demonstrated remarkable performance, securing top-tier rankings in all three lighting-related tracks in the NTIRE 2026 Challenge.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that both specialized and generalized image restoration frameworks can achieve high perceptual quality and generalization, highlighting the significance of dataset-specific and cross-dataset approaches in addressing lighting-related challenges.

title: Optimal last-iterate convergence in matrix games with bandit feedback using the log-barrier
summary: We study the problem of learning minimax policies in zero-sum matrix games. Fiegel et al. (2025) recently showed that achieving last-iterate convergence in this setting is harder when the players are uncoupled, by proving a lower bound on the exploitability gap of Omega(t^{-1/4}). Some online mirror descent algorithms were proposed in the literature for this problem, but none have truly attained this rate yet. We show that the use of a log-barrier regularization, along with a dual-focused analysis, allows this O-tilde(t^{-1/4}) convergence with high-probability. We additionally extend our idea to the setting of extensive-form games, proving a bound with the same rate.

AI.tldr: This paper studies minimax policies in zero-sum games, proposing a method using log-barrier regularization to achieve improved convergence rates.
AI.motivation: The study addresses the challenge of achieving last-iterate convergence in uncoupled zero-sum matrix games, a problem shown to be difficult due to previously established lower bounds on exploitability gaps.
AI.method: The authors utilize a log-barrier regularization technique and a dual-focused analysis to achieve a convergence rate with high probability.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves a convergence rate of O-tilde(t^{-1/4}) in zero-sum matrix games and is extended to extensive-form games with the same rate.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully demonstrates that log-barrier regularization can address the challenges of achieving optimal convergence rates in both matrix and extensive-form games.

title: Where are the Humans? A Scoping Review of Fairness in Multi-agent AI Systems
comment: In proceedings of European Conference on Information Systems (ECIS) 2026
summary: Rapid advances in Generative AI are giving rise to increasingly sophisticated Multi-Agent AI (MAAI) systems. While AI fairness has been extensively studied in traditional predictive scenarios, its examination in MAAI remains nascent and fragmented. This scoping review critically synthesizes existing research on fairness in MAAI systems. Through a qualitative content analysis of 23 selected studies, we identify five archetypal approaches. Our findings reveal that fairness in MAAI systems is often addressed superficially, lacks robust normative foundations, and frequently overlooks the complex dynamics introduced by agent autonomy and system-level interactions. We argue that fairness must be embedded structurally throughout the development lifecycle of MAAI, rather than appended as a post-hoc consideration. Meaningful evaluation requires explicit human oversight, normative clarity, and a precise articulation of fairness objectives and beneficiaries. This review provides a foundation for advancing fairness research in MAAI systems by highlighting critical gaps, exposing prevailing limitations, and suggesting pathways.

AI.tldr: A scoping review identifies key gaps in exploring fairness within sophisticated Multi-Agent AI (MAAI) systems, based on an analysis of 23 studies.
AI.motivation: The study aims to explore and synthesize research on fairness in MAAI systems, an emerging field with limited research and significant challenges.
AI.method: The authors conducted a qualitative content analysis of 23 selected studies on fairness within Multi-Agent AI systems.
AI.result: The review identified five approaches to fairness in MAAI, revealing superficial treatment, lack of normative foundations, and neglect of agent autonomy and interactions.
AI.conclusion: Fairness must be integral to MAAI development rather than an afterthought, requiring human oversight, clear norms, and defined fairness objectives. The study lays a foundation for future research.

title: Fabricator or dynamic translator?
summary: LLMs are proving to be adept at machine translation although due to their generative nature they may at times overgenerate in various ways. These overgenerations are different from the neurobabble seen in NMT and range from LLM self-explanations, to risky confabulations, to appropriate explanations, where the LLM is able to act as a human translator would, enabling greater comprehension for the target audience. Detecting and determining the exact nature of the overgenerations is a challenging task. We detail different strategies we have explored for our work in a commercial setting, and present our results.

AI.tldr: The paper discusses overgenerations by large language models (LLMs) in machine translation, categorizing them and presenting detection strategies and results in a commercial context.
AI.motivation: Addressing overgenerations in LLM-based machine translation, as these differ from neurobabble seen in traditional NMT systems, with potential risks and benefits.
AI.method: Exploration of strategies for identifying and assessing overgenerations produced by LLMs in a commercial setting.
AI.result: Identification of overgeneration types and presentation of results from strategies used to detect and understand these phenomena.
AI.conclusion: Overgenerations in LLMs present unique challenges and opportunities, and the study offers insights into managing them in practical applications.

title: An Analysis of Regularization and Fokker-Planck Residuals in Diffusion Models for Image Generation
comment: Accepted at IJCNN 2026 IEEE. Personal use of this material is permitted. Permission from IEEE must be obtained for all other uses, in any current or future media, including reprinting/republishing this material for advertising or promotional purposes, creating new collective works, for resale or redistribution to servers or lists, or reuse of any copyrighted component of this work in other works
summary: Recent work has shown that diffusion models trained with the denoising score matching (DSM) objective often violate the Fokker--Planck (FP) equation that governs the evolution of the true data density. Directly penalizing these deviations in the objective function reduces their magnitude but introduces a significant computational overhead. It is also observed that enforcing strict adherence to the FP equation does not necessarily lead to improvements in the quality of the generated samples, as often the best results are obtained with weaker FP regularization. In this paper, we investigate whether simpler penalty terms can provide similar benefits. We empirically analyze several lightweight regularizers, study their effect on FP residuals and generation quality, and show that the benefits of FP regularization are available at substantially lower computational cost. Our code is available at https://github.com/OnnoNiemann/fp_diffusion_analysis.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates lightweight regularizers for diffusion models and shows they can reduce Fokker-Planck residuals at lower computational costs compared to prior penalization methods.
AI.motivation: To address the computational overhead of strictly enforcing adherence to the Fokker-Planck equation in diffusion models while maintaining or improving the quality of generated samples.
AI.method: They empirically test lightweight regularization techniques, comparing their effects on Fokker-Planck deviations and sample generation quality.
AI.result: Lightweight regularizers achieve lower Fokker-Planck deviations and preserve high generation quality at significantly reduced computational costs.
AI.conclusion: Simpler penalty terms provide benefits comparable to stronger regularization while being less computationally demanding, making them a practical alternative.

title: Stability and Generalization in Looped Transformers
summary: Looped transformers promise test-time compute scaling by spending more iterations on harder problems, but it remains unclear which architectural choices let them extrapolate to harder problems at test time rather than memorize training-specific solutions. We introduce a fixed-point based framework for analyzing looped architectures along three axes of stability -- reachability, input-dependence, and geometry -- and use it to characterize when fixed-point iteration yields meaningful predictions. Theoretically, we prove that looped networks without recall have countable fixed points and cannot achieve strong input-dependence at any spectral regime, while recall combined with outer normalization reliably produces a regime in which fixed points are simultaneously reachable, locally smooth in the input, and supported by stable backpropagation. Empirically, we train single-layer looped transformers on chess, sudoku, and prefix-sums and find that downstream performance tracks the framework's predictions across tasks and architectural configurations. We additionally introduce internal recall, a novel recall placement variant, and show that it becomes competitive with -- and on sudoku, substantially better than -- standard recall placement once outer normalization is applied.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes looped transformers, focusing on factors that enable efficient test-time computation for complex tasks, and introduces a framework to assess their stability.
AI.motivation: The aim is to address the challenge of designing looped transformers that scale computation based on problem difficulty without overfitting to training solutions.
AI.method: The authors present a fixed-point framework to analyze looped architectures on stability dimensions and conduct experiments on chess, sudoku, and prefix-sums, testing performance with various architectures and introducing a new recall placement method.
AI.result: The proposed framework successfully predicts the performance of different configurations, and the introduced internal recall outperforms standard methods on tasks like sudoku when combined with outer normalization.
AI.conclusion: Looped transformers can effectively scale compute for harder problems when using recall mechanisms and outer normalization, with the fixed-point framework providing valuable design insights.

title: OpenMobile: Building Open Mobile Agents with Task and Trajectory Synthesis
summary: Mobile agents powered by vision-language models have demonstrated impressive capabilities in automating mobile tasks, with recent leading models achieving a marked performance leap, e.g., nearly 70% success on AndroidWorld. However, these systems keep their training data closed and remain opaque about their task and trajectory synthesis recipes. We present OpenMobile, an open-source framework that synthesizes high-quality task instructions and agent trajectories, with two key components: (1) The first is a scalable task synthesis pipeline that constructs a global environment memory from exploration, then leverages it to generate diverse and grounded instructions. and (2) a policy-switching strategy for trajectory rollout. By alternating between learner and expert models, it captures essential error-recovery data often missing in standard imitation learning. Agents trained on our data achieve competitive results across three dynamic mobile agent benchmarks: notably, our fine-tuned Qwen2.5-VL and Qwen3-VL reach 51.7% and 64.7% on AndroidWorld, far surpassing existing open-data approaches. Furthermore, we conduct transparent analyses on the overlap between our synthetic instructions and benchmark test sets, and verify that performance gains stem from broad functionality coverage rather than benchmark overfitting. We release data and code at https://njucckevin.github.io/openmobile/ to bridge the data gap and facilitate broader mobile agent research.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces OpenMobile, an open-source framework for generating high-quality task instructions and trajectories for mobile agents, enabling significant improvement in agent performance.
AI.motivation: Recent mobile agent systems have demonstrated strong performance, but they rely on closed training data and lack transparency in task and trajectory synthesis methods.
AI.method: OpenMobile comprises (1) a task synthesis pipeline utilizing a global environment memory to create diverse instructions, and (2) a policy-switching strategy for generating error-recovery data via alternating learner and expert models.
AI.result: Agents trained with OpenMobile data perform competitively on mobile agent benchmarks, with a fine-tuned model achieving 64.7% success on AndroidWorld, outperforming other open-data methods.
AI.conclusion: OpenMobile bridges the gap in open data for mobile-agent training and offers a transparent, scalable solution for advancing research in this area. Its data is openly available to benefit the community.

title: MADE: A Living Benchmark for Multi-Label Text Classification with Uncertainty Quantification of Medical Device Adverse Events
summary: Machine learning in high-stakes domains such as healthcare requires not only strong predictive performance but also reliable uncertainty quantification (UQ) to support human oversight. Multi-label text classification (MLTC) is a central task in this domain, yet remains challenging due to label imbalances, dependencies, and combinatorial complexity. Existing MLTC benchmarks are increasingly saturated and may be affected by training data contamination, making it difficult to distinguish genuine reasoning capabilities from memorization. We introduce MADE, a living MLTC benchmark derived from {m}edical device {ad}verse {e}vent reports and continuously updated with newly published reports to prevent contamination. MADE features a long-tailed distribution of hierarchical labels and enables reproducible evaluation with strict temporal splits. We establish baselines across more than 20 encoder- and decoder-only models under fine-tuning and few-shot settings (instruction-tuned/reasoning variants, local/API-accessible). We systematically assess entropy-/consistency-based and self-verbalized UQ methods. Results show clear trade-offs: smaller discriminatively fine-tuned decoders achieve the strongest head-to-tail accuracy while maintaining competitive UQ; generative fine-tuning delivers the most reliable UQ; large reasoning models improve performance on rare labels yet exhibit surprisingly weak UQ; and self-verbalized confidence is not a reliable proxy for uncertainty. Our work is publicly available at https://hhi.fraunhofer.de/aml-demonstrator/made-benchmark.

AI.tldr: This paper presents MADE, a dynamic benchmark tailored for multi-label text classification in healthcare, addressing challenges like label imbalance and avoiding training contamination. It evaluates various model architectures and uncertainty quantification methods, identifying trade-offs between accuracy and reliability.
AI.motivation: The authors address the need for robust uncertainty quantification in machine learning for healthcare applications, specifically in multi-label text classification. Existing benchmarks are often compromised by label imbalance, dependencies, and potential contamination, limiting their effectiveness.
AI.method: The authors propose MADE, a continuously updated MLTC benchmark based on adverse event reports, featuring hierarchical labels and strict temporal splits. They evaluate over 20 models in fine-tuning and few-shot settings while systematically assessing various uncertainty quantification methods.
AI.result: Key findings include trade-offs in model performance: fine-tuned decoders excel in accuracy across label distributions; generative fine-tuning offers superior UQ; large reasoning models perform better on rare labels but with weaker UQ; and self-verbalized confidence is unreliable.
AI.conclusion: MADE provides a reliable, contamination-free benchmark for MLTC in healthcare, revealing performance trade-offs and limitations in UQ methods. The work supports the development of more nuanced tools for clinical applications and is available for public use.

title: Boundary-Centric Active Learning for Temporal Action Segmentation
summary: Temporal action segmentation (TAS) demands dense temporal supervision, yet most of the annotation cost in untrimmed videos is spent identifying and refining action transitions, where segmentation errors concentrate and small temporal shifts disproportionately degrade segmental metrics. We introduce B-ACT, a clip-budgeted active learning framework that explicitly allocates supervision to these high-leverage boundary regions. B-ACT operates in a hierarchical two-stage loop: (i) it ranks and queries unlabeled videos using predictive uncertainty, and (ii) within each selected video, it detects candidate transitions from the current model predictions and selects the top-$K$ boundaries via a novel boundary score that fuses neighborhood uncertainty, class ambiguity, and temporal predictive dynamics. Importantly, our annotation protocol requests labels for only the boundary frames while still training on boundary-centered clips to exploit temporal context through the model's receptive field. Extensive experiments on GTEA, 50Salads, and Breakfast demonstrate that boundary-centric supervision delivers strong label efficiency and consistently surpasses representative TAS active learning baselines and prior state of the art under sparse budgets, with the largest gains on datasets where boundary placement dominates edit and overlap-based F1 scores.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes B-ACT, an active learning framework for enhancing temporal action segmentation by focusing supervision on high-leverage action boundary transitions.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the annotation cost and accuracy degradation issues caused by segmentation errors concentrated around action transitions in temporal action segmentation.
AI.method: B-ACT uses a hierarchical two-stage active learning process. In stage one, it ranks and queries videos using predictive uncertainty. In stage two, it identifies transitions using model predictions and selects boundary frames via a novel boundary scoring method.
AI.result: Experiments on GTEA, 50Salads, and Breakfast datasets show that the proposed boundary-centric supervision method improves label efficiency and outperforms existing baselines and state-of-the-art models for sparse budgets.
AI.conclusion: Focusing annotation efforts on action boundaries yields significant performance improvements in metrics for temporal action segmentation while optimizing label efficiency.

title: How Embeddings Shape Graph Neural Networks: Classical vs Quantum-Oriented Node Representations
summary: Node embeddings act as the information interface for graph neural networks, yet their empirical impact is often reported under mismatched backbones, splits, and training budgets. This paper provides a controlled benchmark of embedding choices for graph classification, comparing classical baselines with quantum-oriented node representations under a unified pipeline. We evaluate two classical baselines alongside quantum-oriented alternatives, including a circuit-defined variational embedding and quantum-inspired embeddings computed via graph operators and linear-algebraic constructions. All variants are trained and tested with the same backbone, stratified splits, identical optimization and early stopping, and consistent metrics. Experiments on five different TU datasets and on QM9 converted to classification via target binning show clear dataset dependence: quantum-oriented embeddings yield the most consistent gains on structure-driven benchmarks, while social graphs with limited node attributes remain well served by classical baselines. The study highlights practical trade-offs between inductive bias, trainability, and stability under a fixed training budget, and offers a reproducible reference point for selecting quantum-oriented embeddings in graph learning.

AI.tldr: This paper benchmarks classical and quantum-oriented node embeddings for graph classification under a controlled pipeline.
AI.motivation: The study arises from inconsistencies in reporting the empirical impact of node embeddings, and aims to provide a unified framework for comparison.
AI.method: The authors compare classical and quantum-oriented embeddings using the same backbone, splits, optimization strategies, and evaluation metrics across multiple datasets.
AI.result: Quantum-oriented embeddings show consistent performance gains in structured datasets, while classical baselines excel in social graphs with fewer node attributes.
AI.conclusion: Controlled benchmarks reveal dataset-dependent performance benefits of quantum-oriented embeddings, offering insights into their practical deployment in graph learning.

title: HyperSpace: A Generalized Framework for Spatial Encoding in Hyperdimensional Representations
summary: Vector Symbolic Architectures (VSAs) provide a well-defined algebraic framework for compositional representations in hyperdimensional spaces. We introduce HyperSpace, an open-source framework that decomposes VSA systems into modular operators for encoding, binding, bundling, similarity, cleanup, and regression. Using HyperSpace, we analyze and benchmark two representative VSA backends: Holographic Reduced Representations (HRR) and Fourier Holographic Reduced Representations (FHRR). Although FHRR provides lower theoretical complexity for individual operations, HyperSpaces modularity reveals that similarity and cleanup dominate runtime in spatial domains. As a result, HRR and FHRR exhibit comparable end-to-end performance. Differences in memory footprint introduce additional deployment trade-offs where HRR requires approximately half the memory of FHRR vectors. By enabling modular, system-level evaluation, HyperSpace reveals practical trade-offs in VSA pipelines that are not apparent from theoretical or operator-level comparisons alone.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces HyperSpace, a modular open-source framework for analyzing Vector Symbolic Architectures (VSAs), revealing practical trade-offs for different backend systems like HRR and FHRR.
AI.motivation: To provide a systematic and modular way to analyze and benchmark compositional representations in VSAs, facilitating insights into their trade-offs in runtime and memory.
AI.method: The authors introduced HyperSpace, which decomposes VSA systems into modular operations like encoding and regression, allowing detailed comparative analysis of two VSA backends: HRR and FHRR.
AI.result: HyperSpace showed that while FHRR has lower theoretical complexity in operations, runtime is dominated by similarity and cleanup, leading to comparable performance with HRR. Memory requirements vary, with HRR being more efficient.
AI.conclusion: HyperSpace enables a comprehensive, modular evaluation of VSA pipelines, shedding light on practical trade-offs (e.g., memory usage and runtime) beyond theoretical metrics alone.

title: From Tokens to Steps: Verification-Aware Speculative Decoding for Efficient Multi-Step Reasoning
summary: Speculative decoding (SD) accelerates large language model inference by allowing a lightweight draft model to propose outputs that a stronger target model verifies. However, its token-centric nature allows erroneous steps to propagate. Prior approaches mitigate this using external reward models, but incur additional latency, computational overhead, and limit generalizability. We propose SpecGuard, a verification-aware speculative decoding framework that performs step-level verification using only model-internal signals. At each step, SpecGuard samples multiple draft candidates and selects the most consistent step, which is then validated using an ensemble of two lightweight model-internal signals: (i) an attention-based grounding score that measures attribution to the input and previously accepted steps, and (ii) a log-probability-based score that captures token-level confidence. These signals jointly determine whether a step is accepted or recomputed using the target, allocating compute selectively. Experiments across a range of reasoning benchmarks show that SpecGuard improves accuracy by 3.6% while reducing latency by ~11%, outperforming both SD and reward-guided SD.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SpecGuard, a speculative decoding framework for large language model inference that uses step-level verification via model-internal signals, resulting in improved accuracy and reduced latency compared to existing methods.
